# Andy Gunawardena (Data Science)

Hi, I'm Dr. Andy Ner, a professor of computer science. I'm also the co-chair of the Princeton NJ AI hub. My research group studied the use of AI in education for increasing accessibility and scalability. I teach courses in AI and data science at the senior undergraduate level, often total students exceeding 500 students each semester. The specific courses are data management for data science and introduction to data science. With machine learning, the lecture halls packed with over 250 students at a time. Scaling meaningful learning through traditional teaching is a challenge. That's why we turn to ai. Since, uh, 2022, we have been working with GPD to enhance the learning experience through curated videos by creating huge EPD, our customized area assistant powered by Chad G. Data shows that 86% of students frequently look for instructional videos online. The curated videos from the instructed gives students the power to take control of their learning in the context of the course. Instead of being limited by the pace of a live lecture, they can pause, rewind, and revisit challenging concepts as many times as needed. Any instructor can build a custom curated video collection from a playlist using qubit study AI, and enabled transcription and deep search. As students are moving away from traditional textbooks, curated videos have become their default choice of extended learning. To make it easier for an instructor to custom curate questions for each video, they can use the help of QGPT that can generate first draft of MCQ. Select all or open-ended questions using GPT. Then the instructor can edit the questions, make sure they're accurate and can further enhance the questions. This saves a tremendous amount of time and provides students with a way to actively engage with the videos. The data collected from qubit's dashboard help instructors to assign grades and other extra credit. One of the more popular features of QGPT is that students can ask AI to generate an MCQ relevant to the video they're watching. Students can also respond to the questions and get feedback from QGPT. We believe that students should learn how to critically evaluate AI responses. Therefore, we provide tools for students to give feedback on AI responses. We use the data for our research to see how well AI responds to questions. Students can also ask open-ended questions related to the video they're watching. This allows students to ask questions without having to establish the context as QGPT knows the context of the video they're watching. It's able to answer the question well. One of the other benefits, of course, specific AI is that students get to see what the other students are asking and learn from them. The repo questions generated by students allow our researchers to learn more about where we can improve. How do students use the AI assisted curated videos After class? Most students turn to our AI assisted curated videos to dive deep into the material, clear up any confusion and practice with interactive problems built right into the videos. Our data shows that students don't just watch. They actively engage. They explore the concepts, ask thoughtful questions using QGPT and even extend the discussion beyond what is covered in the video. Based on the service of thousands of students who use curated videos, powered by QGPT since 2022, reports that they find the videos to be the number one or number two most helpful learning resource in the course.
